original_abstract: Copper-based catalysts are promising candidates for the electrochemical conversion of $\text{CO}_2$ into methane, a vital energy source. However, achieving high activity and selectivity remains a challenge due to a lack of fundamental design principles for navigating complex reaction pathways. In this work, we propose a dual-strategy design for carbon-supported Cu catalysts that integrates the atomic-scale tuning of active sites with the mesoscale engineering of the carbon support architecture to create an optimal local microenvironment for methane production. In situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy reveal that under reaction conditions, atomically dispersed $\text{Cu-N}_3$ sites undergo a dynamic reconstruction into nitrogen- and hydroxyl-immobilized $\text{Cu}_3$ ($\text{N,OH-Cu}_3$) clusters. These clusters exhibit a moderate adsorption affinity for *CO and a significantly lowered energy barrier for *CO hydrogenation, effectively favoring the $\text{CO}_2$-to-$\text{CH}_4$ pathway over thermodynamically demanding C-C coupling. Paradoxically, the choice of support is equally critical; we developed a block copolymer-derived carbon fiber featuring interconnected, bicontinuous mesochannels. This unique architecture creates an $\text{H}_2\text{O}$-deficient microenvironment and extends the residence time of CO intermediates, thereby suppressing the competing hydrogen evolution reaction and promoting deep reduction to methane. Consequently, this catalyst achieves an unprecedented Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% for methane at an industrially relevant current density of 300 mA $\text{cm}^{-2}$. This study provides a robust framework for steering reaction pathways in interfacial catalysis by coordinating atomic-level site engineering with mesoscale structural design.
humanized_abstract: The carbon dioxide reduction to methane is highly desired for production of clean energy. Although transition metals such as Cu have been widely used for the catalytic reduction of $\text{CO}_2$, their catalytic performance remains unsatisfactory, which is largely ascribed to a lack of quantitative knowledge of how the catalyst structures and reaction networks can be rationally tailored to achieve selective and efficient reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ into methane. Here, the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction into methane at a high current efficiency using a dual-strategy design of the carbon-supported Cu catalysts is realized. In situ X-ray absorption and Raman spectroscopy reveal the reconstruction of the $\text{Cu-N}_3$ active sites into $\text{N,OH-Cu}_3$ clusters while performing the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction. The binding energies of *CO to the catalytically active sites and the energy barriers for the $\text{CO}$ hydrogenation over the prepared catalysts are significantly lowered than those over the metallic Cu surface and Cu-N3 active sites, suggesting a higher selectivity toward $\text{CO}_2$ reduction to $\text{CH}_4$ than the competitive and highly enthalpy-change-demanding $\text{CO}$ dimerization into $\text{C}_2$ species. It is remarkable that the structure of the carbon supports also has a significant influence on the catalytic performances, and using the block copolymer-derived carbon fiber with the desired morphology of a network of intertwined bicontinuous mesochannels, which endowed the $\text{CO}_2$ reduction into $\text{CH}_4$ with a suppressive $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ and prolonging $\text{CO}$ residence time, thus yields a high Faradaic efficiency of 74.2% at a relatively high current density of 300 mA $\text{cm}^{-2}$. Overall, this demonstration not only realizes the highly efficient reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ into $\text{CH}_4$ at high current densities but also provided quantitative knowledge regarding how the catalyst structures and reaction networks can be rationally tailored to achieve selective and efficient reduction of $\text{CO}_2$ through an atom-scale site engineering strategy coupled with mesoscale structural engineering approach.
